payload.answer: Use a normalized, deduplicated table representation: parse CSV into typed columns (id,name,value), store unique tuples in a primary/deduped table (or a dimension+fact design if repeated names/ids are common), and index on id (and optionally (id,name)). Preserve duplicates only if they encode distinct events or provenance; otherwise store multiplicity or remove redundant repeats.
payload.decision: Store as a normalized, deduplicated table (fact table + optional dimension tables) rather than as a raw verbatim CSV blob; index on id (and optionally composite {id,name}) and keep value as a typed field, preserving duplicates only if they carry semantic meaning.

entity_resolution.reason: The fixture identifier provides the scope of the input dataset for the storage-policy decision.

payload.intent: Determine an appropriate storage strategy for a CSV table (fixture: large_table_500_rows_001) consisting of 500 rows with repeated {id,name,value} patterns, and compile Brain memory accordingly.

memory_cards.statement: For a large repeated CSV table (500 rows) with repeated {id,name,value} tuples, prefer a normalized representation with deduplication, indexing, and typed columns, unless duplicates are semantically meaningful.

relationships.object: whether duplicates are redundant or events
relationships.subject: {id,name,value}

memory_cards.statement: If repeated rows are purely redundant (same id/name/value with no additional metadata like timestamps or provenance), deduplication is appropriate; if repetitions represent distinct events, keep a row-level event table or include a multiplicity/provenance field.

Represent as a fact table with columns (id, name, value) using appropriate types (e.g., value numeric/text), add primary key or surrogate key, and create indexes on id and optionally (id,name) to speed lookups.

details: Interpreted fixture as a repeated-row CSV and selected a deduped normalized storage policy.
Flagged the ambiguity about whether duplicates carry semantic meaning.

repair_options: If duplicates represent distinct events, add an event identifier or timestamp/provenance column and store duplicates in an append-only event table.
If value type is ambiguous, infer and store value as numeric when consistent; otherwise store as text with a separate typed_value field when possible.
If id is not unique, use a composite key (id,name) or introduce a surrogate key plus a uniqueness constraint only when duplicates are confirmed redundant.
